Deep Learning for Climate
Prediction: A Survey
John Smith1, Jane Doe2
1MIT 2Stanford
Abstract We present a comprehensive
survey of deep learning methods
applied to climate prediction tasks.
Recent advances in neural networks
have enabled significant progress in
weather forecasting and long-term
climate modeling.
1 Introduction Climate change is
one of the most pressing challenges
facing humanity today. Traditional
numerical weather prediction (NWP)
models require enormous computational
resources. Machine learning offers
promising alternatives that can
complement existing approaches.
Recent work by Chen et al. (2023)
demonstrated that transformer-based
architectures can achieve comparable
accuracy to NWP models while
reducing inference time by orders
of magnitude. However, challenges
remain in handling the inherent
multi-scale nature of atmospheric
dynamics.
2 Related Work Several groups have
explored neural approaches to weather
prediction. Pathak et al. (2022)
introduced FourCastNet, a Fourier
neural operator-based model. Lam
et al. (2023) presented GraphCast,
achieving state-of-the-art results.
3 Methods We propose a hybrid
architecture combining convolutional
and attention mechanisms. Our model
operates on a latitude-longitude
grid with 0.25 degree resolution.
4 Results Our model achieves
a RMSE of 0.42 on the ERA5
benchmark dataset. Table 1 shows
detailed results across variables.
5 Conclusion We have shown that
deep learning methods can effectively
model climate dynamics. Future work
will explore ensemble approaches.
References
[1] Chen et al., 2023. Pangu-Weather.
[2] Pathak et al., 2022. FourCastNet.
[3] Lam et al., 2023. GraphCast.
